title: Dry skin and cracked lips in cold weather
excerpt: When cold, dry winter air strips moisture from your skin and lips, Ayurveda has a precise explanation: Shishira ritu intensifies Vata dosha. Here is how to bring balance back using kitchen staples and gentle daily rituals.
body_html:
why winter feels like it is drying you from the inside out

You notice it first on your lips, that tight, papery feeling when you wake up on a cold morning. Then the knuckles start to crack, the shins get flaky, and no amount of water seems to help. If this is a familiar late-winter pattern for you, Ayurveda would say your body is simply reflecting the season around it.

In Ayurvedic thinking, every season carries a dominant quality. The classical texts describe two cold-season phases: Hemanta (early winter, roughly November to January) and Shishira (late winter, roughly January to March). The seasons are not identical in their effect on the body. During Hemanta, Agni (digestive fire) is strong and the body tends to build strength; the dominant dosha accumulating is Kapha. It is in Shishira that Vata dosha, the principle of air and space, rises to dominance. Vata is dry, light, cold, rough, and mobile, and when those qualities intensify in the environment, the same qualities build up in the body (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3).

The Sanskrit term for this kind of dryness is Rukshata, and it is one of the primary signs of Vata aggravation. Cracked lips and flaking skin appear when the body's natural moisture, called Sneha or internal unctuousness, begins to diminish. When Sneha diminishes, tissues lose their suppleness. The dedicated classical discussion of oleation therapy is found in Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 13 (Snehadhyaya), which explains how maintaining Sneha in the tissues is the foundation for preventing this kind of deterioration.

When Sneha diminishes, tissues lose their suppleness.

what Ayurveda recommends for the cold season

The classical winter regimen, called Hemanta-Shishira Ritucharya, advises increasing foods and practices that are unctuous (Snigdha), heavy, and warming. In Shishira in particular, when Vata is dominant, this warming and oleating approach directly counters the dry and cold qualities that cause skin and lip dryness (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3).

Internally, warm foods cooked in ghee or sesame oil are encouraged. A small spoon of pure ghee with warm milk before bed is a classic recommendation for maintaining tissue moisture. Soups and kichadi made with cumin, ginger, and coriander keep digestion strong while adding warmth. When Agni is well-supported, the body is better able to nourish its outer tissues, including the skin (Caraka Samhita, Chikitsa Sthana).

Externally, daily oil application before bathing, a practice called Abhyanga, is one of the most consistently recommended habits in the classical literature. Sesame oil is the first choice for Vata-type dryness because it is warming and penetrating. The Ashtanga Hridaya specifically lists Abhyanga among daily practices that maintain the skin's health and suppleness, improve circulation, and pacify Vata (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 2). Even five minutes of warm oil rubbed onto the limbs and lips before a shower makes a noticeable difference over a few weeks.

everyday habits that help, starting tonight

You do not need a complicated protocol. The Ayurvedic approach to winter skin care is almost entirely built from things already in an Indian kitchen. Ghee on the lips at bedtime is both a classical remedy and genuinely effective as an occlusive barrier. A thin film of sesame oil on the hands and feet before wearing socks to bed helps cracked heels and dry knuckles respond quickly.

Warm water is preferred over cold through the day, both for drinking and washing. Cold water contracts tissues and aggravates Vata; warm water supports circulation and keeps the skin's environment hydrated from within. The Bhavaprakasha also emphasizes the use of warm water for maintaining general health in cold seasons (Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda).

For the face and lips, a simple paste of fresh cream (malai) and a drop of ghee applied at night is a traditional home remedy that aligns with the Snehana principle. Avoid harsh soaps and long hot showers, both of which strip the skin's natural oils and worsen Rukshata. A brief warm shower followed immediately by oil application while the skin is still slightly damp seals moisture most effectively.

Sesame seeds in your food, a pinch of cumin and coriander in your morning warm water, and a cup of ginger tea in the afternoon are small additions that support internal warmth and digestion, keeping Vata from overwhelming the tissues.

when to see a doctor

The dryness and cracking described here is common and usually responds well to seasonal care within a few weeks. However, some situations deserve professional attention. See a doctor or a qualified Ayurvedic vaidya if your skin develops deep painful fissures that bleed or show signs of infection, if the dryness is extreme and appears suddenly rather than seasonally, if you notice other symptoms alongside dry skin such as unusual fatigue, hair loss, weight gain, or feeling cold all the time (which can indicate thyroid conditions), or if lips remain cracked despite consistent care for more than three to four weeks.

This article is general information for a healthy adult and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. If you have an existing skin condition such as eczema or psoriasis, or are on medication that affects skin health, consult your physician before making changes to your routine.

Try This Today

  • Ghee on lips at bedtime: Dab a small amount of pure cow ghee onto your lips before sleeping. It acts as an occlusive barrier overnight and aligns directly with the Snehana principle of external oleation.
  • Five-minute Abhyanga before your shower: Warm a small amount of sesame oil between your palms and rub it into your arms, legs, and hands. Leave for five minutes, then take a warm (not hot) shower. Do this daily through the Shishira months.
  • Warm water through the day: Switch cold water for warm or room-temperature water in winter. Cold water contracts tissues and aggravates Vata; warm water supports circulation and internal moisture.
  • Ghee in morning food: Add one small spoon of ghee to your morning meal, whether roti, dal, or kichadi. This supports internal Sneha and keeps tissues nourished from within.
  • Ginger-cumin tea in the afternoon: Simmer a thin slice of ginger and a pinch of cumin in water for five minutes. Sip warm. This supports Agni (digestive fire), which helps the body channel nutrients to outer tissues including the skin.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Ginger (अदरक)

Warming and Vata-pacifying; supports Agni in cold months.

Cumin (जीरा)

Digestive and warming; helps channel nourishment to outer tissues.

Coriander (धनिया)

Balances digestion without overheating; pairs well with ginger in winter teas.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: When cold, dry winter air strips moisture from your skin and lips, Ayurveda has a precise explanation: Shishira ritu intensifies Vata dosha. Here is how to bring balance back using kitchen staples and gentle daily rituals.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Shishira (late winter) intensifies Vata dosha, and its dry, cold qualities cause Rukshata (dryness) in skin and lips; early-winter Hemanta is distinct and dominated by Kapha accumulation.
Daily sesame oil application before bathing (Abhyanga) is the most direct classical remedy for Vata-driven winter dryness.
Ghee internally and topically, warm water through the day, and warming spices in food address the root cause from multiple angles.

glossary_terms.definition: One of the three doshas; governs movement, air, and space; associated with dryness, coldness, and lightness.
Dryness; one of the primary qualities of aggravated Vata in the tissues.
Internal unctuousness or oleation; the natural moisture and lubrication that keeps tissues supple.
Self-oil massage; a daily practice of applying warm oil to the body before bathing.
Seasonal regimen; Ayurvedic guidelines for diet and lifestyle that change with the seasons.
Digestive fire; the metabolic force that processes food and sustains all bodily tissues.

sources: Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 2 (Dinacharya, on Abhyanga)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3 (Ritucharya, on Hemanta and Shishira regimen)
Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 13 (Snehadhyaya, on Snehana and tissue oleation)
Caraka Samhita, Chikitsa Sthana (on Agni and tissue nourishment)
Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda (on seasonal warm-water use)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Vata" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Vata is defined as: One of the three doshas; governs movement, air, and space; associated with dryness, coldness, and lightness.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to ghee on lips at bedtime. Dab a small amount of pure cow ghee onto your lips before sleeping. It acts as an occlusive barrier overnight and aligns directly with the Snehana principle of external oleation.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Ginger for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Ginger is highly regarded. Warming and Vata-pacifying; supports Agni in cold months.
